(Redirected from [Thomas Babington Macaulay, 1st Baron Macaulay](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Babington_Macaulay,_1st_Baron_Macaulay&redirect=no "Thomas Babington Macaulay, 1st Baron Macaulay"))

British historian and politician (1800–1859)

For another person with the name, see [Thomas Babington Macaulay (Nigeria)](/wiki/Thomas_Babington_Macaulay_(Nigeria) "Thomas Babington Macaulay (Nigeria)").

"Baron Macaulay" redirects here. For the British Labour politician, see [Donald Macaulay, Baron Macaulay of Bragar](/wiki/Donald_Macaulay,_Baron_Macaulay_of_Bragar "Donald Macaulay, Baron Macaulay of Bragar").

"Thomas Macaulay" redirects here. For other uses, see [Thomas Macaulay (disambiguation)](/wiki/Thomas_Macaulay_(disambiguation) "Thomas Macaulay (disambiguation)").

**Thomas Babington Macaulay, 1st Baron Macaulay**, [PC](/wiki/Privy_Council_(United_Kingdom) "Privy Council (United Kingdom)"), [FRS](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society "Fellow of the Royal Society"), [FRSE](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh "Fellow of the Royal Society of Edinburgh") ([/ˈbæbɪŋtən məˈkɔːli/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); 25 October 1800 – 28 December 1859) was a British historian, poet and [Whig](/wiki/Whig_(British_political_faction) "Whig (British political faction)") politician who served as the [Secretary at War](/wiki/Secretary_at_War "Secretary at War") between 1839 and 1841, and as the [Paymaster General](/wiki/Paymaster_General "Paymaster General") between 1846 and 1848. He is best known for his *[The History of England](/wiki/The_History_of_England_from_the_Accession_of_James_the_Second "The History of England from the Accession of James the Second")*, a seminal example of [Whig history](/wiki/Whig_history "Whig history") which expressed Macaulay's belief in the [inevitability of sociopolitical progress](/wiki/Idea_of_progress "Idea of progress") and has been widely commended for its prose style.[[1]](#cite_note-MacKenzie2013-1) Macaulay also played a substantial role in determining [India's education policy](/wiki/History_of_education_in_the_Indian_subcontinent#Reform "History of education in the Indian subcontinent").

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Babington_Macaulay&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Macaulay was born at [Rothley Temple](/wiki/Rothley_Temple "Rothley Temple")[[2]](#cite_note-2) in [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire") on 25 October 1800, the son of [Zachary Macaulay](/wiki/Zachary_Macaulay "Zachary Macaulay"), a Scottish [Highlander](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands"), who became a [colonial governor](/wiki/Governor#British_Empire_and_Commonwealth_of_Nations "Governor") and [abolitionist](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Abolitionism in the United Kingdom"), and [Selina Mills](/wiki/Selina_Mills "Selina Mills") of [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol"), a former pupil of [Hannah More](/wiki/Hannah_More "Hannah More").[[3]](#cite_note-3) They named their first child after his uncle [Thomas Babington](/wiki/Thomas_Babington "Thomas Babington"), a [Leicestershire](/wiki/Leicestershire "Leicestershire") landowner and politician,[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuper2009146-5) who had married Zachary's sister Jean.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight18678-6) The young Macaulay was noted as a child prodigy; as a toddler, gazing out of the window from his cot at the chimneys of a local factory, he is reputed to have asked his father whether the smoke came from the fires of hell.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan201021-7)

He was educated at a private school in [Hertfordshire](/wiki/Hertfordshire "Hertfordshire"), and, subsequently, at [Trinity College, Cambridge](/wiki/Trinity_College,_Cambridge "Trinity College, Cambridge"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) where he won several prizes, including the [Chancellor's Gold Medal](/wiki/Chancellor%27s_Gold_Medal "Chancellor's Gold Medal") in June 1821,[[9]](#cite_note-ODNB-9) and where he in 1825 published a prominent essay on [Milton](/wiki/John_Milton "John Milton") in the *[Edinburgh Review](/wiki/Edinburgh_Review "Edinburgh Review")*. Macaulay did not study classical literature while at Cambridge, though he subsequently did when he was in India. In his letters he describes his reading of the *[Aeneid](/wiki/Aeneid "Aeneid")* whilst he was in Malvern in 1851, and says he was moved to tears by [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil")'s poetry.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalton186923-10) He taught himself German, Dutch and Spanish, and was fluent in French.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan20109-11) He studied law and in 1826 he was [called to the bar](/wiki/Called_to_the_bar "Called to the bar"), before he took more interest in a political career.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPattison1911193-12) Macaulay in the *Edinburgh Review* in 1827, and in a series of anonymous letters to *[The Morning Chronicle](/wiki/The_Morning_Chronicle "The Morning Chronicle")*,[[13]](#cite_note-Rupprecht-13) censured the analysis of indentured labour by the British Colonial Office expert [Colonel Thomas Moody, Kt.](/wiki/Thomas_Moody_(British_Army_officer) "Thomas Moody (British Army officer)")[[13]](#cite_note-Rupprecht-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) Macaulay's evangelical Whig father [Zachary Macaulay](/wiki/Zachary_Macaulay "Zachary Macaulay"), who desired a 'free black peasantry' rather than equality for Africans,[[15]](#cite_note-Interest-15) also censured, in the *[Anti-Slavery Reporter](/wiki/Anti-Slavery_Reporter "Anti-Slavery Reporter")*, Moody's contentions.[[13]](#cite_note-Rupprecht-13)

Macaulay, who did not marry nor have children, was rumoured to have fallen in love with [Maria Kinnaird](/wiki/Maria_Kinnaird "Maria Kinnaird"), who was the wealthy ward of [Richard 'Conversation' Sharp](/wiki/Richard_Sharp_(politician) "Richard Sharp (politician)").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECropper1864-16) Macaulay's strongest emotional relationships were with his youngest sisters: Margaret, who died while he was in India, and Hannah, to whose daughter Margaret, whom he called 'Baba', he was also attached.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2010466-17)

## India (1834–1838)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Babington_Macaulay&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: India (1834–1838)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Thomas_Babington_Macaulay%2C_Baron_Macaulay_by_John_Partridge.jpg/250px-Thomas_Babington_Macaulay%2C_Baron_Macaulay_by_John_Partridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Babington_Macaulay,_Baron_Macaulay_by_John_Partridge.jpg)

Macaulay by John Partridge

Macaulay in 1830 accepted the invitation of [the Marquess of Lansdowne](/wiki/Henry_Petty-FitzMaurice,_3rd_Marquess_of_Lansdowne "Henry Petty-FitzMaurice, 3rd Marquess of Lansdowne") that he become Member of Parliament for the [pocket borough](/wiki/Rotten_and_pocket_boroughs "Rotten and pocket boroughs") of [Calne](/wiki/Calne_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Calne (UK Parliament constituency)"). Macaulay's maiden speech in Parliament advocated abolition of the [civil disabilities of the Jews in the UK](/wiki/Disabilities_(Jewish) "Disabilities (Jewish)"). Macaulay's subsequent speeches in favour of parliamentary reform were commended.[[9]](#cite_note-ODNB-9) He became MP for [Leeds](/wiki/Leeds_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Leeds (UK Parliament constituency)")[[9]](#cite_note-ODNB-9) subsequent to the 1833 enactment of the [Reform Act 1832](/wiki/Reform_Act_1832 "Reform Act 1832"), by which Calne's representation was reduced from two MPs to one, and by which Leeds, which had not been represented before, had two MPs. Macaulay remained grateful to his former patron, Lansdowne, who remained his friend.

Macaulay was [Secretary to the Board of Control](/wiki/Secretary_to_the_Board_of_Control "Secretary to the Board of Control") under [Lord Grey](/wiki/Charles_Grey,_2nd_Earl_Grey "Charles Grey, 2nd Earl Grey") from 1832 until he in 1833 required, as a consequence of the penury of his father, a more remunerative office, than that of [the unremunerated office of an MP](/wiki/Salaries_of_members_of_the_United_Kingdom_Parliament#Historical_salaries "Salaries of members of the United Kingdom Parliament"), from which he resigned after the passing of the [Government of India Act 1833](/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1833 "Government of India Act 1833") to accept an appointment as first Law Member of the [Governor-General's Council](/wiki/Council_of_India "Council of India"). In 1834 Macaulay went to India, where he served on the Supreme Council between 1834 and 1838.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2002260-18)
His [*Minute on Indian Education*](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Minutes_on_Education_In_India,_Written_in_the_Years_1835,_1836_and_1837/Minute_by_Mr._Macaulay_(2_February,_1835) "s:Minutes on Education In India, Written in the Years 1835, 1836 and 1837/Minute by Mr. Macaulay (2 February, 1835)") of February 1835 was primarily responsible for the introduction of Western institutional education to India.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Macaulay recommended the introduction of the English language as the [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of secondary education instruction in all schools where there had been none before, and the training of English-speaking Indians as teachers.[[1]](#cite_note-MacKenzie2013-1)
In his minute, he urged [Lord William Bentinck](/wiki/Lord_William_Bentinck "Lord William Bentinck"), the then-[Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_India "Governor-General of India") to reform secondary education on [utilitarian](/wiki/Utilitarian "Utilitarian") lines to deliver "useful learning", a phrase that to him was synonymous with Western culture. There was no tradition of secondary education in vernacular languages; the institutions supported by the [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") taught either in [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") or [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Hence, he argued, "We have to educate a people who cannot at present be educated by means of their mother-tongue. We must teach them some foreign language." Macaulay argued that Sanskrit and Persian were no more accessible than English to the speakers of the Indian vernacular languages and existing Sanskrit and Persian texts were of little use for "useful learning". In one of the less scathing passages of the Minute he [wrote](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Minutes_on_Education_In_India,_Written_in_the_Years_1835,_1836_and_1837/Minute_by_Mr._Macaulay_(2_February,_1835)#LanguageWorthKnowing "s:Minutes on Education In India, Written in the Years 1835, 1836 and 1837/Minute by Mr. Macaulay (2 February, 1835)"):

> I have no knowledge of either Sanskrit or Arabic. But I have done what I could to form a correct estimate of their value. I have read translations of the most celebrated Arabic and Sanskrit works. I have conversed both here and at home with men distinguished by their proficiency in the Eastern tongues. I am quite ready to take the Oriental learning at the valuation of the Orientalists
> themselves. I have never found one among them who could deny that a single shelf of a good European library was worth the whole native literature of India and Arabia.

He further [argued](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Minutes_on_Education_In_India,_Written_in_the_Years_1835,_1836_and_1837/Minute_by_Mr._Macaulay_(2_February,_1835)#OrientalPoetryAndHistory "s:Minutes on Education In India, Written in the Years 1835, 1836 and 1837/Minute by Mr. Macaulay (2 February, 1835)"):

> It will hardly be disputed, I suppose, that the department of literature in which the Eastern writers stand highest is poetry. And I certainly never met with any orientalist who ventured to maintain that the Arabic and Sanskrit poetry could be compared to that of the great European nations. But when we pass from works of imagination to works in which facts are recorded and general principles investigated, the superiority of the Europeans becomes absolutely immeasurable. It is, I believe, no exaggeration to say that all the historical information which has been collected from all the books written in the Sanskrit language is less valuable than what may be found in the most paltry abridgments used at preparatory schools in England. In every branch of physical or moral philosophy, the relative position of the two nations is nearly the same.

Hence, from the sixth year of schooling onwards, instruction should be in European learning, with English as the medium of instruction. This would create a class of anglicised Indians who would serve as cultural intermediaries between the British and the Indians; the creation of such a class was necessary before any reform of vernacular education. He [stated](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Minutes_on_Education_In_India,_Written_in_the_Years_1835,_1836_and_1837/Minute_by_Mr._Macaulay_(2_February,_1835)#ClassOfIndiansAsInterpreters "s:Minutes on Education In India, Written in the Years 1835, 1836 and 1837/Minute by Mr. Macaulay (2 February, 1835)"):

> I feel with them that it is impossible for us, with our limited means, to attempt to educate the body of the people. We must at present do our best to form a class who may be interpreters between us and the millions whom we govern; a class of persons Indian in blood and colour, but English in tastes, in opinions, in morals, and in intellect. To that class we may leave it to refine the vernacular dialects of the country, to enrich those dialects with terms of science borrowed from the Western nomenclature, and to render them by degrees fit vehicles for conveying knowledge to the great mass of the population.

Macaulay's largely coincided with Bentinck's views[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpear193878–101-21) and Bentinck's English Education Act 1835 closely matched Macaulay's recommendations (in 1836, a school named [La Martinière](/wiki/La_Martiniere_Calcutta "La Martiniere Calcutta"), founded by Major General Claude Martin, had one of its houses named after him), but subsequent Governors-General took a more conciliatory approach to existing Indian education.

His final years in India were devoted to the creation of a Penal Code, as the leading member of the Law Commission. In the aftermath of the [Indian Mutiny of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Mutiny_of_1857 "Indian Mutiny of 1857"), Macaulay's criminal law proposal was enacted.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Indian Penal Code](/wiki/Indian_Penal_Code "Indian Penal Code") in 1860 was followed by the Criminal Procedure Code in 1872 and the [Civil Procedure Code](/wiki/Code_of_Civil_Procedure_(India) "Code of Civil Procedure (India)") in 1908. The [Indian Penal Code](/wiki/Indian_Penal_Code "Indian Penal Code") inspired counterparts in most other [British colonies](/wiki/British_colonies "British colonies"), and to date many of these laws are still in effect in places as far apart as [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") and [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe"), as well as in [India](/wiki/India "India") itself until recently.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) This includes Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, which remains the basis for [laws which criminalize homosexuality](/wiki/LGBT_rights_by_country_or_territory "LGBT rights by country or territory") in several [Commonwealth nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In Indian culture, the term "Macaulay's Children" is sometimes used to refer to people born of Indian ancestry who adopt Western culture as a lifestyle, or display [attitudes influenced by colonialism](/wiki/Colonial_mentality#The_Indian_Subcontinent "Colonial mentality") ("[Macaulayism](/wiki/Macaulayism "Macaulayism")")[[25]](#cite_note-25) – expressions used disparagingly, and with the implication of disloyalty to one's country and one's heritage. In independent India, Macaulay's idea of the [civilising mission](/wiki/Civilising_mission "Civilising mission") has been used by Dalitists, in particular by [neo-liberalist](/wiki/Neo-liberalist "Neo-liberalist")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Chandra Bhan Prasad](/wiki/Chandra_Bhan_Prasad "Chandra Bhan Prasad"), as a "creative appropriation for self-empowerment", based on the view that the Dalit community was empowered by Macaulay's deprecation of Hindu culture and support for Western-style education in India.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWattMann201123-26)

Domenico Losurdo states that "Macaulay acknowledged that the English colonists in India behaved like [Spartans](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta") confronting *[helots](/wiki/Helot "Helot")*: we are dealing with 'a race of sovereign' or a 'sovereign caste', wielding absolute power over its 'serfs'."[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELosurdo2014250-27) Losurdo noted that this did not prompt any doubts from Macaulay over the right of Britain to administer its colonies in an autocratic fashion; for example, while Macaulay described the administration of [governor-general of India](/wiki/Governor-general_of_India "Governor-general of India") [Warren Hastings](/wiki/Warren_Hastings "Warren Hastings") as being so despotic that "all the injustice of former oppressors, Asiatic and European, appeared as a blessing", he (Hastings) deserved "high admiration" and a rank among "the most remarkable men in our history" for "having saved England and civilisation".[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELosurdo2014250–251-28)

### Return to British public life (1838–1857)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Babington_Macaulay&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Return to British public life (1838–1857)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Thomas_Babington_Macaulay%2C_Baron_Macaulay_by_Sir_Francis_Grant.jpg/250px-Thomas_Babington_Macaulay%2C_Baron_Macaulay_by_Sir_Francis_Grant.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Babington_Macaulay,_Baron_Macaulay_by_Sir_Francis_Grant.jpg)

Macaulay by Sir Francis Grant

Returning to Britain in 1838, he became MP again in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh (UK Parliament constituency)") in the following year. He was made [Secretary at War](/wiki/Secretary_at_War "Secretary at War") in 1839 by [Lord Melbourne](/wiki/Lord_Melbourne "Lord Melbourne") and was sworn of the [Privy Council](/wiki/Her_Majesty%27s_Most_Honourable_Privy_Council "Her Majesty's Most Honourable Privy Council") the same year.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In 1841 Macaulay addressed the issue of [copyright](/wiki/Copyright "Copyright") law. Macaulay's position, slightly modified, became the basis of [copyright law](/wiki/Copyright_law "Copyright law") in the English-speaking world for many decades.[[30]](#cite_note-asu-30) Macaulay argued that copyright is a monopoly and as such has generally negative effects on society.[[30]](#cite_note-asu-30) After the fall of Melbourne's government in 1841 Macaulay devoted more time to literary work, and returned to office as [Paymaster General](/wiki/Paymaster_General "Paymaster General") in 1846 in [Lord John Russell](/wiki/Lord_John_Russell "Lord John Russell")'s administration.

In the election of 1847 he lost his seat in Edinburgh.[[31]](#cite_note-31) He attributed the loss to the anger of religious zealots over his speech in favour of expanding the annual government grant to [Maynooth College](/wiki/Maynooth_College "Maynooth College") in Ireland, which trained young men for the Catholic priesthood; some observers also attributed his loss to his neglect of local issues. In 1849 he was elected [Rector of the University of Glasgow](/wiki/Rector_of_the_University_of_Glasgow "Rector of the University of Glasgow"), a position with no administrative duties, often awarded by the students to men of political or literary fame.[[32]](#cite_note-32) He also received the [freedom of the city](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_city "Freedom of the city").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In 1852, the voters of Edinburgh offered to re-elect him to Parliament. He accepted on the express condition that he need not campaign and would not pledge himself to a position on any political issue. Remarkably, he was elected on those terms.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Due to ill health, he seldom attended the House. His weakness after suffering a heart attack caused him to postpone for several months making his speech of thanks to the Edinburgh voters. He resigned his seat in January 1856.[[34]](#cite_note-Lord_Macaulay-34) In 1857 he was raised to the [peerage](/wiki/Peerage "Peerage") as **Baron Macaulay**, of [Rothley](/wiki/Rothley "Rothley") in the [County of Leicester](/wiki/County_of_Leicester "County of Leicester"),[[35]](#cite_note-35) but seldom attended the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords").[[34]](#cite_note-Lord_Macaulay-34)

### Later life (1857–1859)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Babington_Macaulay&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Later life (1857–1859)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/The_Funeral_of_Thomas_Babington_Macaulay%2C_Baron_Macaulay_by_Sir_George_Scharf.jpg/250px-The_Funeral_of_Thomas_Babington_Macaulay%2C_Baron_Macaulay_by_Sir_George_Scharf.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Funeral_of_Thomas_Babington_Macaulay,_Baron_Macaulay_by_Sir_George_Scharf.jpg)

*The Funeral of Thomas Babington Macaulay, Baron Macaulay*, by Sir [George Scharf](/wiki/George_Scharf "George Scharf")

Macaulay sat on the committee to decide on the historical subjects to be painted in the new [Palace of Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster").[[36]](#cite_note-36) The need to collect reliable portraits of notable figures from history for this project led to the foundation of the [National Portrait Gallery](/wiki/National_Portrait_Gallery_(United_Kingdom) "National Portrait Gallery (United Kingdom)"), which was formally established on 2 December 1856.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Macaulay was amongst its founding trustees and is honoured with one of only three busts above the main entrance.

During his later years his health made work increasingly difficult for him. He died of a heart attack on 28 December 1859, aged 59, leaving his major work, *The History of England from the Accession of James the Second* incomplete.[[38]](#cite_note-38) On 9 January 1860 he was buried in [Westminster Abbey](/wiki/Westminster_Abbey "Westminster Abbey"), in [Poets' Corner](/wiki/Poets%27_Corner "Poets' Corner"),[[39]](#cite_note-39) near a statue of [Addison](/wiki/Joseph_Addison "Joseph Addison").[[9]](#cite_note-ODNB-9) As he had no children, his peerage became extinct on his death.

Macaulay's nephew, [Sir George Trevelyan, Bt](/wiki/Sir_George_Trevelyan,_2nd_Baronet "Sir George Trevelyan, 2nd Baronet"), wrote the "Life and Letters" of his uncle. His great-nephew was the Cambridge historian [G. M. Trevelyan](/wiki/G._M._Trevelyan "G. M. Trevelyan").

## Literary works

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Babington_Macaulay&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Literary works")]

As a young man he composed the ballads *Ivry* and *The Armada*,[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1881-40) which he later included as part of *[Lays of Ancient Rome](/wiki/Lays_of_Ancient_Rome "Lays of Ancient Rome")*, a series of very popular poems about heroic episodes in Roman history which he began composing in India and continued in Rome, finally publishing in 1842.[[41]](#cite_note-Sullivan-41) The most famous of them, *[Horatius](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Horatius "s:Horatius")*, concerns the heroism of [Horatius Cocles](/wiki/Horatius_Cocles "Horatius Cocles"). It contains the oft-quoted lines:[[42]](#cite_note-42)

> Then out spake brave Horatius,  
> The Captain of the Gate:  
> "To every man upon this earth  
> Death cometh soon or late.  
> And how can man die better  
> Than facing fearful odds,  
> For the ashes of his fathers,  
> And the temples of his gods?"

His essays, originally published in the *[Edinburgh Review](/wiki/Edinburgh_Review "Edinburgh Review")*, were collected as *[Critical and Historical Essays](/wiki/Critical_and_Historical_Essays_(Macaulay) "Critical and Historical Essays (Macaulay)")* in 1843.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1941x-43)

## Historian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Babington_Macaulay&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Historian")]

During the 1840s, Macaulay undertook his most famous work, *[The History of England from the Accession of James the Second](/wiki/The_History_of_England_from_the_Accession_of_James_the_Second "The History of England from the Accession of James the Second")*, publishing the first two volumes in 1848. At first, he had planned to bring his history down to the reign of [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III"). After publication of his first two volumes, his hope was to complete his work with the death of [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain") in 1714.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1848Vol._V,_title_page_and_prefatory_"Memoir_of_Lord_Macaulay"-44)

The third and fourth volumes, bringing the history to the [Peace of Ryswick](/wiki/Peace_of_Ryswick "Peace of Ryswick"), were published in 1855. At his death in 1859 he was working on the fifth volume. This, bringing the *History* down to the death of [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England"), was prepared for publication by his sister, Lady Trevelyan, after his death.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacaulay1848-45)

## Political writing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Babington_Macaulay&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Political writing")]

Macaulay's political writings are famous for their ringing prose and for their confident, sometimes dogmatic, emphasis on a progressive model of British history, according to which the country threw off superstition, autocracy and confusion to create a balanced constitution and a forward-looking culture combined with freedom of belief and expression. This model of human progress has been called the [Whig interpretation of history](/wiki/Whig_interpretation_of_history "Whig interpretation of history"). This philosophy appears most clearly in the essays Macaulay wrote for the *[Edinburgh Review](/wiki/Edinburgh_Review "Edinburgh Review")* and other publications, which were collected in book form and a steady best-seller throughout the 19th century. But it is also reflected in *History*; the most stirring passages in the work are those that describe the "[Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution")" of 1688.

Macaulay's approach has been criticised by later historians for its one-sidedness and its complacency. [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") referred to him as a 'systematic falsifier of history'.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarx1906788Ch._XXVII-46) His tendency to see history as a drama led him to treat figures whose views he opposed as if they were villains, while characters he approved of were presented as heroes. Macaulay goes to considerable length, for example, to absolve his main hero [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") of any responsibility for the [Glencoe massacre](/wiki/Glencoe_massacre "Glencoe massacre"). [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill") devoted a [four-volume biography](/wiki/Marlborough:_His_Life_and_Times "Marlborough: His Life and Times") of the [Duke of Marlborough](/wiki/Duke_of_Marlborough "Duke of Marlborough") to rebutting Macaulay's slights on his ancestor, expressing hope "to fasten the label 'Liar' to his genteel coat-tails".[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChurchill1947132-47) Later historians have also highlighted his views on non-European cultures and philosophies as explicitly racist, citing, for example, his remark that 'a single shelf of a good European library was worth the whole native literature of India and Arabia'.

## Legacy as a historian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Babington_Macaulay&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Legacy as a historian")]

The Liberal historian [Lord Acton](/wiki/Lord_Acton "Lord Acton") read Macaulay's *History of England* four times and later described himself as "a raw English schoolboy, primed to the brim with Whig politics" but "not [Whiggism](/wiki/Whiggism "Whiggism") only, but Macaulay in particular that I was so full of." However, Acton would later find fault in Macaulay.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHill201125-48) In 1880 Acton classed Macaulay (with [Burke](/wiki/Edmund_Burke "Edmund Burke") and [Gladstone](/wiki/Gladstone "Gladstone")) as one "of the three greatest Liberals".[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaul190457-49) In 1883, he advised [Mary Gladstone](/wiki/Mary_Gladstone "Mary Gladstone"):

> [T]he Essays are really flashy and superficial. He was not above par in literary criticism; his Indian articles will not hold water; and his two most famous reviews, on [Bacon](/wiki/Francis_Bacon "Francis Bacon") and [Ranke](/wiki/Leopold_von_Ranke "Leopold von Ranke"), show his incompetence. The essays are only pleasant reading, and a key to half the prejudices of our age. It is the *History* (with one or two speeches) that is wonderful. He knew nothing respectably before the seventeenth century, he knew nothing of foreign history, of religion, philosophy, science, or art. His account of debates has been thrown into the shade by Ranke, his account of diplomatic affairs, by [Klopp](/wiki/Onno_Klopp "Onno Klopp"). He is, I am persuaded, grossly, basely unfair. Read him therefore to find out how it comes that the most unsympathetic of critics can think him very nearly the greatest of English writers...[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaul1904173-50)

In 1885, Acton asserted that:

> We must never judge the quality of a teaching by the quality of the Teacher, or allow the spots to shut out the sun. It would be unjust, and it would deprive us of nearly all that is great and good in this world. Let me remind you of Macaulay. He remains to me one of the greatest of all writers and masters, although I think him utterly base, contemptible and odious for certain reasons which you know.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPaul1904210-51)

In 1888, Acton wrote that Macaulay "had done more than any writer in the literature of the world for the propagation of the Liberal faith, and he was not only the greatest, but the most representative, Englishman then living".[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELord_Acton1919482-52)

[W. S. Gilbert](/wiki/W._S._Gilbert "W. S. Gilbert") described Macaulay's wit, "who wrote of [Queen Anne](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain")" as part of Colonel Calverley's Act I patter song in the libretto of the 1881 operetta *[Patience](/wiki/Patience_(opera) "Patience (opera)")*. (This line may well have been a joke about the Colonel's pseudo-intellectual bragging, as most educated Victorians knew that Macaulay did *not* write of Queen Anne; the *History* encompasses only as far as the death of William III in 1702, who was succeeded by Anne.)

[Herbert Butterfield](/wiki/Herbert_Butterfield "Herbert Butterfield")'s *The Whig Interpretation of History* (1931) attacked Whig history. The Dutch historian [Pieter Geyl](/wiki/Pieter_Geyl "Pieter Geyl"), writing in 1955, considered Macaulay's *Essays* as "exclusively and intolerantly English".[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeyl195830-53)

On 7 February 1954, [Lord Moran](/wiki/Charles_Wilson,_1st_Baron_Moran "Charles Wilson, 1st Baron Moran"), doctor to the Prime Minister, Sir [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"), recorded in his diary:

> [Randolph](/wiki/Randolph_Churchill "Randolph Churchill"), who is writing a life of the late Lord Derby for [Longman](/wiki/Longman "Longman")'s, brought to luncheon a young man of that name. His talk interested the P.M. ... Macaulay, Longman went on, was not read now; there was no demand for his books. The P.M. grunted that he was very sorry to hear this. Macaulay had been a great influence in his young days.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELord_Moran1968553–554-54)

Churchill wrote in [his autobiography](/wiki/My_Early_Life "My Early Life") that he "accepted all Macaulay wrote as gospel".[[55]](#cite_note-55)

[George Richard Potter](/wiki/George_Richard_Potter "George Richard Potter"), Professor and Head of the Department of History at the [University of Sheffield](/wiki/University_of_Sheffield "University of Sheffield") from 1931 to 1965, stated "In an age of long letters ... Macaulay's hold their own with the best".[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPotter195910-56) However Potter also stated:

> For all his linguistic abilities he seems never to have tried to enter into sympathetic mental contact with the classical world or with the Europe of his day. It was an insularity that was impregnable ... If his outlook was insular, however, it was surely British rather than English.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPotter195925-57)

With regards to Macaulay's determination to inspect physically the places mentioned in his *History*, Potter said:

> Much of the success of the famous third chapter of the *History* which may be said to have introduced the study of [social history](/wiki/Social_history "Social history"), and even ... [local history](/wiki/Local_history "Local history"), was due to the intense local knowledge acquired on the spot. As a result it is a superb, living picture of Great Britain in the latter half of the seventeenth century ... No description of the [relief of Londonderry](/wiki/Siege_of_Derry "Siege of Derry") in a major history of England existed before 1850; after his visit there and the narrative written round it no other account has been needed ... Scotland came fully into its own and from then until now it has been a commonplace that [English history](/wiki/English_history "English history") is incomprehensible without Scotland.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPotter195929-58)

Potter noted that Macaulay has had many critics, some of whom put forward some salient points about the deficiency of Macaulay's *History* but added: "The severity and the minuteness of the criticism to which the *History of England* has been subjected is a measure of its permanent value. It is worth every ounce of powder and shot that is fired against it." Potter concluded that "in the long roll of English historical writing from [Clarendon](/wiki/Edward_Hyde,_1st_Earl_of_Clarendon "Edward Hyde, 1st Earl of Clarendon") to [Trevelyan](/wiki/G._M._Trevelyan "G. M. Trevelyan") only [Gibbon](/wiki/Edward_Gibbon "Edward Gibbon") has surpassed him in security of reputation and certainty of immortality".[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPotter195935-59)

[Piers Brendon](/wiki/Piers_Brendon "Piers Brendon") wrote that Macaulay is "the only British rival to Gibbon."[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrendon2010126-60) In 1972, J.R. Western wrote that: "Despite its age and blemishes, Macaulay's *History of England* has still to be superseded by a full-scale modern history of the period."[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWestern1972403-61) In 1974 [J. P. Kenyon](/wiki/J._P._Kenyon "J. P. Kenyon") stated that: "As is often the case, Macaulay had it exactly right."[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKenyon197447n._14-62)

[W. A. Speck](/wiki/W._A._Speck "W. A. Speck") wrote in 1980, that a reason Macaulay's *History of England* "still commands respect is that it was based upon a prodigious amount of research".[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeck198057-63) Speck stated:

> Macaulay's reputation as an historian has never fully recovered from the condemnation it implicitly received in Herbert Butterfield's devastating attack on *The Whig Interpretation of History*. Though he was never cited by name, there can be no doubt that Macaulay answers to the charges brought against Whig historians, particularly that they study the past with reference to the present, class people in the past as those who furthered progress and those who hindered it, and judge them accordingly.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeck198064-64)

According to Speck:

> [Macaulay too often] denies the past has its own validity, treating it as being merely a prelude to his own age. This is especially noticeable in the third chapter of his *History of England*, when again and again he contrasts the backwardness of 1685 with the advances achieved by 1848. Not only does this misuse the past, it also leads him to exaggerate the differences.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeck198064-64)

On the other hand, Speck also wrote that Macaulay "took pains to present the virtues even of a rogue, and he painted the virtuous warts and all",[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeck198065-65) and that "he was never guilty of suppressing or distorting evidence to make it support a proposition which he knew to be untrue".[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeck198067-66) Speck concluded:

> What is in fact striking is the extent to which his *History of England* at least has survived subsequent research. Although it is often dismissed as inaccurate, it is hard to pinpoint a passage where he is categorically in error ... his account of events has stood up remarkably well ... His interpretation of the [Glorious Revolution](/wiki/Glorious_Revolution "Glorious Revolution") also remains the essential starting point for any discussion of that episode ... What has not survived, or has become subdued, is Macaulay's confident belief in progress. It was a dominant creed in the era of the [Great Exhibition](/wiki/Great_Exhibition "Great Exhibition"). But [Auschwitz](/wiki/Auschwitz "Auschwitz") and [Hiroshima](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki") destroyed this century's claim to moral superiority over its predecessors, while the exhaustion of natural resources raises serious doubts about the continuation even of material progress into the next.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeck198067-66)

In 1981, [J. W. Burrow](/wiki/J._W._Burrow "J. W. Burrow") argued that Macaulay's *History of England*:

> ... is not simply partisan; a judgement, like that of [Firth](/wiki/Charles_Harding_Firth "Charles Harding Firth"), that Macaulay was always the Whig politician could hardly be more inapposite. Of course Macaulay thought that the Whigs of the seventeenth century were correct in their fundamental ideas, but the hero of the *History* was William, who, as Macaulay says, was certainly no Whig ... If this was [Whiggism](/wiki/Whiggism "Whiggism") it was so only, by the mid-nineteenth century, in the most extended and inclusive sense, requiring only an acceptance of parliamentary government and a sense of gravity of precedent. [Butterfield](/wiki/Herbert_Butterfield "Herbert Butterfield") says, rightly, that in the nineteenth century the Whig view of history became the English view. The chief agent of that transformation was surely Macaulay, aided, of course, by the receding relevance of seventeenth-century conflicts to contemporary politics, as the power of the crown waned further, and the [civil disabilities of Catholics](/wiki/Civil_disabilities_of_Catholics "Civil disabilities of Catholics") and [Dissenters](/wiki/English_Dissenters "English Dissenters") were removed by legislation. The *History* is much more than the vindication of a party; it is an attempt to insinuate a view of politics, pragmatic, reverent, essentially [Burkean](/wiki/Burkean "Burkean"), informed by a high, even tumid sense of the worth of public life, yet fully conscious of its interrelations with the wider progress of society; it embodies what [Hallam](/wiki/Henry_Hallam "Henry Hallam") had merely asserted, a sense of the privileged possession by Englishmen of their history, as well as of the epic dignity of government by discussion. If this was sectarian it was hardly, in any useful contemporary sense, polemically Whig; it is more like the sectarianism of English respectability.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrow1983-67)

In 1982, [Gertrude Himmelfarb](/wiki/Gertrude_Himmelfarb "Gertrude Himmelfarb") wrote:

> [M]ost professional historians have long since given up reading Macaulay, as they have given up writing the kind of history he wrote and thinking about history as he did. Yet there was a time when anyone with any pretension to cultivation read Macaulay.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHimmelfarb1986163-68)

Himmelfarb also laments that "the history of the *History* is a sad testimonial to the cultural regression of our times".[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHimmelfarb1986165-69)

In the novel *[Marathon Man](/wiki/Marathon_Man_(novel) "Marathon Man (novel)")* and its [film adaptation](/wiki/Marathon_Man_(film) "Marathon Man (film)"), the protagonist was named 'Thomas Babington' after Macaulay.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldman197420-70)

*The Quarrel of Croker and Macaulay,* by historian [William Thomas](/wiki/William_Thomas_(historian) "William Thomas (historian)") *(*published in 2000), studies the relationship between the Anglo-Irish politician and author John Wilson [Croker](/wiki/John_Wilson_Croker "John Wilson Croker") and Macaulay.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

In 2008, [Walter Olson](/wiki/Walter_Olson "Walter Olson") argued for the pre-eminence of Macaulay as a British [classical liberal](/wiki/Classical_liberal "Classical liberal").[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOlson2008309–310-72)
